---
title: "A Math-Heavy Quarto Post"
description: "A compact example with macros, equation numbering, theorem blocks, and cross references."
date: 2026-07-15
categories: [quarto, math]
author: "Haotian Li"
---

## Inline and Display Math

Inline notation works with dollar signs, for example $f : \R^d \to \R$ and $\E[X]$.

Display equations are written with double dollar signs. Add an identifier like `{#eq-risk}` after the equation to number and reference it:

$$
R(\theta) = \E_{(X,Y) \sim \mathcal{D}}
\left[
  \ell\{f_\theta(X), Y\}
\right].
$$ {#eq-risk}

We can refer back to @eq-risk in prose.

## Aligned Equations

Use standard LaTeX environments for multi-line derivations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_\theta R(\theta)
&= \nabla_\theta \E[\ell(f_\theta(X), Y)] \\
&= \E[\nabla_\theta \ell(f_\theta(X), Y)].
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-gradient}

The interchange in @eq-gradient needs regularity assumptions, which is a nice place to use a theorem block.

## Definitions and Theorems

::: {#def-lipschitz}
## Lipschitz Function

A function $f : \R^d \to \R$ is $L$-Lipschitz if, for all $x,y \in \R^d$,

$$
|f(x) - f(y)| \le L \norm{x-y}.
$$
:::

Refer to @def-lipschitz anywhere after the block.

::: {#thm-contraction}
## Contraction Mapping

Let $(M,d)$ be a complete metric space. If $T : M \to M$ satisfies

$$
d(Tx, Ty) \le c\,d(x,y)
\quad \text{for some } 0 \le c < 1,
$$

then $T$ has a unique fixed point.
:::

@thm-contraction is one of the most useful fixed-point tools.

::: {.proof}
Choose any $x_0 \in M$ and define $x_{n+1} = T x_n$. Then

$$
d(x_{n+1}, x_n) \le c^n d(x_1, x_0).
$$

The sequence is Cauchy, hence converges to some $x^\star \in M$. Continuity of $T$ gives $T x^\star = x^\star$. Uniqueness follows by applying the contraction inequality to two fixed points.
:::

## Useful Macro Examples

The project-wide MathJax configuration in `_quarto.yml` defines a few shortcuts:

$$
\inner{x}{y} = x^\top y,
\qquad
\norm{x}_2 = \sqrt{\inner{x}{x}},
\qquad
\theta^\star = \argmin_{\theta \in \R^d} R(\theta).
$$ {#eq-macros}

That keeps your posts readable while still rendering proper LaTeX.

## References {#sec-references}

You can cite equations like @eq-macros, definitions like @def-lipschitz, theorems like @thm-contraction, and sections like @sec-references.
